definitions: The cervical spine consists of seven vertebrae (C1-C7) forming the bony framework of the neck.
It supports the skull, allows dynamic multi-axial neck range of motion, and houses the ascending/descending spinal tract networks.
coreConcepts: Atypical vertebrae include C1 (Atlas - no vertebral body or spinous process) and C2 (Axis - carries the dens/odontoid process of vertebral body extension).
Typical vertebrae (C3-C6) have bifid spinous processes, triangular vertebral foramina, and foramina transversaria carrying vertebral arteries.
C7 (Vertebra prominens) features a long, non-bifid spinous process that sits as the primary anatomical projection at the base of neck.
equipment: Pressure biofeedback goniometer for neck ROM

pearls: The transverse ligament of the atlas holds the odontoid process of C2 against the anterior arch of C1. Injury here (e.g., in rheumatoid arthritis or high force trauma) triggers atlanto-axial subluxation and fatal spinal cord compression.
precautions: Perform the vertebral artery test (Hallpike/VBI check) prior to any end-range cervical rotation manual drills.
indications: Evaluation of cervical radiculopathy (disc herniations / osteophytic spurs)

general: High-velocity cervical thrust manipulation in vertebral-basilar insufficiency (VBI)

treatmentProcedure: Palpate the prominent spinous process of C7.
Grasp and palpate the transverse processes of the Atlas (C1) between the mastoid process and mandible ramus.
Verify cervical rotation ROM centering axis over the crown of head.

mechanism: Vertebral arteries run through C6-C1 transverse foramina to enter the cranial cavity.
Smooth bony margins prevent arterial occlusion during dynamic cervical movement.

mcqs.question: Which cervical joint is primarily responsible for the rotation of the head (the 'No' movement)?
mcqs.explanation: The atlanto-axial pivot joint allows extensive rotation of the skull, which produces approximately 50-60% of total neck rotation range.

viva.question: Which cervical vertebrae does not have a vertebral body or a spinous process? Atlas (C1).
What structures pass through the cervical transversarium foramina? Vertebral artery, vertebral vein, and sympathetic plexus.

references: Norkin & Levangie's Joint Structure and Function - Chapter 4 (The Vertebral Column)
Chaurasia B.D. Textbook of Anatomy - Vol 3

parameters.Nerve Roots Count: 8 cervical nerve roots (C1-C8 emerging above respective bone segments, save C8)
